35: A fulfillment: Philip 'beginning from this Scripture proclaimed good news about Jesus' — the Ethiopian's question about Isaiah 53:7-8 is answered by identifying the silent suffering lamb as Jesus. This is the apostolic hermeneutic in miniature: the Suffering Servant is not Israel in general or Isaiah himself but the specific individual who died silently and was raised ('his life is taken away from the earth' → resurrection reversal). The Christological reading of Isa 53 as a resurrection narrative (the servant suffers, is cut off, but the Lord 'prolongs his days', Isaiah 53:10 MT) is already present in Jesus's own self-understanding (Luke 22:37) and becomes the template for apostolic preaching throughout Acts.
1: The great persecution that breaks out 'on that day' — the day of Stephen's stoning — is the first major test of the resurrection claim. Saul's approval of the killing places him at the epicenter of anti-Christ violence, which Luke will use as the foil for one of history's most dramatic reversals (Acts 9). Christologically, the scattering of the church is a consequence of the same forces that killed Jesus; but as the cross became the instrument of redemption, so persecution becomes the instrument of mission: those scattered 'went about preaching the word' (Acts 8:4). The death of the proto-martyr Stephen is absorbed into the same pattern as the death of Christ — suffering produces scattering, scattering produces proclamation.
2: The burial of Stephen by 'devout men' who mourned deeply (epoiēsanto kopeton megan ep' autō) echoes the burial of Jesus by Joseph of Arimathea and the mourning women. The liturgy of honoring the martyred body is itself a Christological act — it confesses that the body matters, that death is real, and that burial is the form faithful witness takes when it cannot yet see the resurrection. Stephen's burial is also a public act of solidarity with the Way at a moment of maximum danger: those who buried him identified themselves as his community.
3: Saul's systematic destruction of the church — house by house, dragging away men and women — presents the persecutor-figure in his fullest anti-type form. The women's inclusion is notable (women were full members of the persecuted community). Christologically, this is the moment before the sunrise: the forces that killed Jesus are now targeting his body, the church. The very intensity of Saul's violence against Christ's community will make his conversion (Acts 9) the most powerful evidence that the resurrection is real — no psychology of gradual persuasion can account for it.
4: A fulfillment: 'Those who were scattered went about proclaiming the word.' The cross-and-resurrection pattern — death → scattering → proclamation — is the missional logic of Acts. What the persecutors intend as the extermination of the movement is divinely reordered into its expansion. This is not merely historical optimism; it is the Christological claim that Christ's pattern of death-into-life cannot be permanently reversed by any human power. The scattering of believers is the sowing of the gospel seed (John 12:24 — 'unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit').
5: A fulfillment: Philip's proclamation of 'the Messiah' in Samaria is the extension of Christ's own Samaritan mission (John 4; Luke 17:11-19). Samaria was the theological borderland — neither pure Jew nor Gentile — where Jesus had already planted seeds (John 4:38-39, 'Others have labored, and you have entered into their labor'). Now Philip reaps what Jesus sowed. Ezekiel's vision of the reunification of Judah and Ephraim (Ezekiel 37:15-28) and Isaiah's promise of the northern territories receiving the Servant's light (Isaiah 9:1-2) are being enacted. The Samaritan mission is the penultimate stage of Acts 1:8 before the Gentile breakthrough.
6: The crowds' unified attention to Philip's message is authenticated by signs (sēmeia). The signs-and-wonders of the apostolic mission continue the program that Jesus announced in Luke 4:18-21 (Isaiah 61:1 — 'good news to the poor, healing to the brokenhearted, freedom for the captives'). The crowd's attention (prosechein) to 'what Philip was saying' is to the message about the Messiah, not merely to the miracles. The signs serve the Word; they point beyond themselves to the person of Christ whose power they display.
7: A direct fulfillment: impure spirits cast out with shrieks, the paralyzed and lame healed — this is the liberation program of Luke 4:18 enacted in Samaria. The exorcisms demonstrate Christ's victory over demonic powers (Luke 10:18, 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven'; Colossians 2:15, 'disarming the rulers and authorities'). Each healing of paralysis and lameness echoes Jesus's own healing acts (Luke 5:17-26; Acts 7:22) and Isaiah's messianic vision: 'then the lame will leap like a deer' (Isaiah 35:6). Philip's ministry is not his own but the continuation of Christ's ministry through the Spirit.
8: The great joy (chara megalē) that results is messianic joy — the same language used of the angels' announcement to the shepherds (Luke 2:10) and of the disciples' return from the mission (Luke 10:17). Isaiah 52:7-10 envisioned the messenger of good news bringing joy to Jerusalem; here the messianic joy breaks out in Samaria. Philip's preaching of the Messiah produces in Samaria what the Messiah's own birth produced in Bethlehem: great joy. The geographic expansion of joy is itself a christological datum — the risen Christ's benefit is not spatially restricted.
9: Simon the sorcerer's introduction sets up the Christological contrast that will dominate Acts 8:9-24. Simon presented himself through his powers, attracting attention to himself ('this man is the Great Power of God,' Acts 8:10). The Messiah, by contrast, came in self-giving humility, identifying with the least and dying for the last. Simon is a counterfeit messianic figure — he appropriates the title 'Power of God' (cf. 1 Corinthians 1:24, 'Christ the power of God') — but exercises it for self-exaltation rather than self-sacrifice. The gospel always encounters counterfeit versions of its own claims.
10: 'This man is the Great Power of God' (Hē dynamis tou theou hē kaloumenē Megalē): Simon is acclaimed with a Christological title. Paul's Christology in 1 Corinthians 1:24 calls Christ 'the power of God and the wisdom of God.' Simon's followers applied the same title to a sorcerer — which is the deepest form of the Christological contrast: between the one who exercises power through death-and-resurrection and the one who exercises power through self-display. The Samaritans' belief in Philip (Acts 8:12) is the conversion from false 'power of God' to the true Messiah whose power is cruciform.
11: Simon's sorcery had held the city captive 'for a long time' (hikanos chronos) — an extended spiritual bondage that the gospel of the Messiah now breaks. The liberation from Simon's domination is itself a Christological act: Christ came to 'bind the strong man' (Luke 11:21-22), and Philip's mission in Samaria is an enactment of that binding. The power of Christ's name proves stronger than the power of a popular sorcerer, which answers the question implicit in every Samaritan's experience: which power is real?
12: A direct revelation: 'When they believed Philip as he proclaimed the good news of the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized.' The formula 'kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ' is Luke's shorthand for the full gospel — the universal reign of God and its specific bearer. Baptism into 'the name of Jesus Christ' (Acts 2:38) is the ritual entry into Christ's own death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4): to be baptized into the name is to be baptized into the person. The Samaritans' baptism represents a major expansion of the covenant community through the crucified-risen Lord.
13: Simon's belief and baptism is presented without editorializing at this point — Luke lets the narrative expose the inadequacy of his faith gradually. The pattern is important Christologically: genuine encounter with the risen Christ transforms the will (as Paul's will was transformed on the Damascus road); Simon's fascination with 'signs and great miracles' (Acts 8:13b) reveals that he is drawn to the power-display rather than to the person who exercises that power. Christological faith is not admiration for miracles but surrender to the Lord who performs them.
14: The Jerusalem apostles send Peter and John to Samaria — the apostolic oversight maintains the unity of the body of Christ across geographic expansion. That the first Jewish-Samaritan breach in the church's mission requires direct apostolic mediation shows how significant the crossing of this historic divide was. The sending of Peter and John echoes Jesus's own sending of seventy disciples (Luke 10:1) and reflects the risen Christ's continued organization of his mission through appointed emissaries.
15: Peter and John pray specifically 'that they might receive the Holy Spirit.' The Spirit is here understood as the gift of the risen Christ (Acts 2:33 — 'having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing'). The apostles' prayer acknowledges that the Spirit's coming is not automatic upon faith and baptism but is the sovereign gift of the exalted Lord. Their intercession is itself a christological act — they address the Father, asking for the Son's gift, in expectation that Christ will extend his Pentecostal giving to Samaria.
16: The Spirit's non-arrival before the apostles' laying on of hands is one of Acts' more puzzling features. The Christological explanation that best fits Luke's narrative: the Spirit's delayed arrival serves to visibly connect the Samaritan church to the Jerusalem community, so that the first extension of the gospel beyond Jerusalem's borders does not produce a separated Samaritan Christianity. The risen Christ, through the Spirit, maintains the unity of his body even as it expands geographically. The delay is not a withholding but a timing that serves ecclesial Christology.
17: A direct revelation: 'Then they laid their hands on them, and they received the Holy Spirit.' The Spirit's coming upon the Samaritans through the apostolic act is the experiential proof that Christ's Pentecostal promise (Acts 1:5, 'you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit') is not restricted to Israel. Joel 2:28 — 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh' — is being enacted step by step across Acts: first Jerusalem Jews, then Samaritans, then Gentiles (Acts 10). The Spirit's presence is the mark of Christ's ownership: those on whom he pours his Spirit belong to him.
18: Simon's offer of money for the Spirit's gift is the defining moment that reveals his misunderstanding of what the Spirit is. The Spirit is the personal gift of the risen Christ — an extension of his own person to his people (John 14:16-17, 'another Helper'). To treat the Spirit as a purchasable commodity is to treat Christ's person as a commodity. The word 'simony' (the buying and selling of church offices) derives from Simon's name — his error became so paradigmatic that it names the entire category of spiritual commercialism.
19: 'Give me also this ability so that everyone on whom I lay my hands may receive the Holy Spirit.' Simon wants the capacity to convey the Spirit — not the Spirit himself, not relationship with Christ, but the instrumental power. This is the religious entrepreneur's temptation: to harness spiritual power for one's own program rather than to submit to the Lord who gives the Spirit to whom he wills. Christologically, Simon's request inverts the proper order: the Spirit is not a power to be wielded but the Lord who wields his servants.
20: Peter's rebuke — 'may your silver perish with you' (to argyrion sou syn soi eiē eis apōleian) — uses judgment language (cf. Deuteronomy 13:15 LXX, where those who lead Israel into idolatry are devoted to destruction). The offer to purchase God's gift treats the grace of the cross as a commodity — which is the deepest possible offense against Christ's atoning work. The gospel is 'bought without price' (Isaiah 55:1) precisely because Christ paid the price no human could pay; to offer money for it is to deny the cross.
21: 'Your heart is not right before God' (hē kardia sou ouk estin eutheia enanti tou theou) is the Christological diagnosis: Simon has been exposed to the gospel but not transformed by it. The criterion 'right before God' is not moral performance but the posture of genuine surrender to Christ as Lord. Simon's heart remains self-oriented — he wants the power for his own use. This is why repentance (Acts 8:22) is possible but not yet evident: the issue is not sins committed but a heart that has not been repositioned toward Christ.
22: 'Repent of this wickedness and pray to the Lord in the hope that he may forgive you for having such a thought in your heart.' Peter's appeal is striking: even this egregious error is potentially forgivable through repentance toward Christ. The same gospel that Simon wanted to commercialize is now offered to him personally: repentance and prayer toward 'the Lord' (the risen Christ). This is the gospel's own logic — it is precisely for those whose hearts are not yet right that forgiveness through Christ is offered. The conditional 'if perhaps' (ei ara) reflects the uncertainty about whether Simon will genuinely repent, not uncertainty about whether Christ forgives those who do.
23: 'For I see that you are full of bitterness and captive to sin.' The 'gall of bitterness' (cholē pikrias) echoes Deuteronomy 29:18 LXX, where apostasy from the covenant produces 'a root bearing gall and bitterness'; and Acts 13:10, where Elymas is described as 'full of deceit and all villainy.' The OT background makes Simon's condition a covenant-faithlessness diagnosis: he has heard the gospel of the Messiah and used it for self-advancement, which is covenant betrayal. 'Captive to sin' (syndesmō adikias) echoes Isaiah 58:6 LXX — the very verse about the Lord's liberation program that stands behind Luke 4:18.
24: Simon's response — 'Pray to the Lord for me' — is ambiguous: it may be genuine fear without genuine repentance (he asks others to pray rather than praying himself), or it may be the beginning of repentance. Luke leaves the resolution open. The early church (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus) identified Simon as the founder of Gnosticism and made him a thoroughly sinister figure; but Luke's narrative is more restrained, leaving Simon's fate unresolved. The Christological point is preserved regardless: the Spirit cannot be purchased, and the Christ who forgives sin requires genuine turning of the heart.
25: After their ministry in Samaria, Peter and John 'returned to Jerusalem, proclaiming the good news to many villages of the Samaritans as they went.' The road back to Jerusalem becomes an ongoing mission in itself — the apostles cannot travel through Samaritan territory without proclaiming the Messiah. Christ's missionary mandate (Acts 1:8) transforms every journey into a proclamation opportunity. The Samaritan mission is expanding even as its first phase concludes: the gospel moves through every interaction, not only formal proclamation events.
26: The angel's direction to Philip — 'Go south to the road ... that goes down from Jerusalem to Gaza' — is divine orchestration of the moment when the gospel will cross a major ethnic and social threshold. The desert road is an unlikely place for a divine appointment; the Christological point is that Christ's mission does not follow human logic of efficiency or strategic planning. The 'desert road' (erēmos) echoes the wilderness context of John the Baptist's preparation (Luke 3:4) and the Israelite sojourn — the desert is a place of divine encounter and testing where the Word is heard.
27: A fulfillment: the Ethiopian eunuch is precisely the figure Isaiah 56:3-5 promises will be included: 'Let not the eunuch say, "Behold, I am a dry tree"... I will give them an everlasting name that shall not be cut off.' Deuteronomy 23:1 had excluded eunuchs from 'the assembly of the LORD'; Isaiah 56 reversed this exclusion for those who kept the Sabbath and held fast to the covenant. The eunuch's reading of Isaiah is providentially appropriate: he is reading the prophetic scroll that contains the promise of his own inclusion. Christ's mission enacts this inclusion — the gospel reaches those whom the Law's cultic provisions had excluded.
28: The eunuch was reading Isaiah the prophet — specifically, the Servant Songs (Acts 8:32-33 cite Isaiah 53:7-8). This is not random; God has orchestrated the encounter around a specific text that contains the key to understanding Jesus's death and resurrection. The Spirit who directed Philip to this road (Acts 8:29) had arranged for the eunuch to be reading the precise passage through which the gospel of the Messiah would be explained. The OT text is not merely background for the Christological proclamation; it is the primary vehicle through which the Spirit connects this seeker to Jesus.
29: 'The Spirit told Philip, “Go to that chariot and stay near it.”' The Spirit's specific direction — not a general prompting but a particular command to a particular vehicle — is the active guidance of the missionary Christ through his Spirit. This is the apostolic pattern throughout Acts: the Spirit directs, the missionary obeys, the mission expands. The Spirit here is not a vague spiritual atmosphere but Christ's personal agent, directing his servant to the specific encounter that will extend the covenant community to Africa.
30: Philip 'ran up' (prosdramōn) to the chariot — eager obedience to the Spirit's command. Hearing the eunuch reading Isaiah aloud (ancient reading was done vocally), Philip's question — 'Do you understand what you are reading?' — is the perfect opening because the answer is 'no' and the question honors the eunuch's genuine seeking. The Christological dimension: Philip does not begin with Jesus but with the text the eunuch is already reading. The apostolic approach follows the Spirit's preparatory work rather than imposing an external message on an unprepared hearer.
31: 'How can I unless someone guides me?' The eunuch's humble admission of interpretive need is his greatest readiness. He has been seeking (the pilgrimage to Jerusalem, the purchase of an expensive Isaiah scroll, the reading on the journey home) — and his seeking has brought him to the precise text that requires a teacher. Christologically, this is the fulfillment of Jesus's promise: 'You will be taught by God' (John 6:45, citing Isaiah 54:13). The Spirit has prepared the seeking; now he provides the teacher. The eunuch invites Philip in — hospitality to the interpreter enables reception of the Messiah.
32: A direct fulfillment: the passage being read is Isaiah 53:7-8 — 'He was led like a sheep to the slaughter, and as a lamb before its shearer is silent, so he did not open his mouth.' The silent lamb is Jesus before Pilate (Luke 23:9 — 'but he gave him no answer'; Matthew 27:12-14 — 'he made no reply'); the silence is one of the most specific fulfillment details in the Passion narrative. Philip will identify the Servant as Jesus — confirming that the NT church's earliest hermeneutic was that Isaiah 53 describes the Messiah's death and that Jesus's silence in the Passion fulfills the sheep-before-shearers image with literal precision.
33: A direct fulfillment: 'In his humiliation he was deprived of justice. Who can speak of his descendants? For his life was taken from the earth.' Three elements of Isaiah 53:8 LXX map onto Jesus: (1) 'humiliation' (tapeinōsis) — the trial before Pilate, the mockery by soldiers, the crucifixion (Philippians 2:8 — 'he humbled himself, becoming obedient to death'); (2) 'deprived of justice' — the kangaroo-court of the Sanhedrin, the washing of Pilate's hands; (3) 'his life was taken from the earth' — the crucifixion, which Isaiah 53:10-12 will reverse: 'he will see his offspring... he will prolong his days.' The 'taking away' of life from the earth anticipates the restoration of that life in the resurrection.
34: The eunuch's hermeneutical question — 'who is the prophet talking about, himself or someone else?' — is the central question of OT interpretation. Philip's answer (Acts 8:35) establishes that the referent is Jesus, not Isaiah himself, not collective Israel, and not an anonymous righteous sufferer. This is the apostolic hermeneutic: the OT prophets were writing about the Messiah who was to come, and their deepest predictions converge on Jesus's death and resurrection. Peter makes the same point explicitly (1 Peter 1:10-12 — 'the prophets who prophesied about the grace that was to be yours searched and enquired carefully, enquiring what person or time the Spirit of Christ in them was indicating').
36: 'Look, here is water! What can prevent me from being baptized?' The eunuch's spontaneous recognition of baptism's availability — drawing the connection between Philip's Christological explanation and the sacramental entry into Christ — shows genuine faith (Acts 8:37). The question 'what prevents me?' (ti kōlyei) echoes the same formula that will appear in Peter's recounting of the Cornelius episode (Acts 10:47 — 'can anyone withhold water from these people?'). The 'nothing prevents' answer is the Christological declaration: faith in Jesus Christ removes every barrier — ethnic, physical, geographic — from the covenant community.
37: A direct revelation: 'I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God.' This verse (a Western text addition, absent from Alexandrian manuscripts) makes explicit the Christological confession that precedes baptism. Whether original or a scribal clarification of the implicit logic, it correctly identifies the baptismal faith: 'Jesus Christ is the Son of God' is the NT confession that most comprehensively names who Jesus is — the Messiah (Christos) and the divine Son (huios tou theou). Baptism into Christ is baptism into this identity: union with the one who is both Messiah and Lord.
38: The baptism itself — both going down into the water — enacts what Paul will explain theologically in Romans 6:3-4: 'Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were therefore buried with him through baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may live a new life.' The eunuch's physical descent into the water and re-emergence from it is his participation in Christ's own death and resurrection. The desert road becomes a Jordan — the place of new-covenant crossing into the Messiah's community.
39: 'The Spirit of the Lord suddenly took Philip away' (pneuma kyriou hērpasen ton Philippon): the translation of Philip echoes Elijah's Spirit-carried transports (1 Kings 18:12; 2 Kings 2:16). The eunuch's response — 'he went on his way rejoicing' (eporeueto tēn hodon autou chairōn) — is the joy of the one who has received the Messiah (cf. Acts 8:8 — the Samaritan joy). He continues his journey to Ethiopia, carrying the gospel with him — the first known African Christian. Isaiah 56:4-5's promise to the eunuch ('I will give them ... an everlasting name') is fulfilled: the man who could not continue his 'name' biologically carries the Name of Jesus to the ends of the earth.
40: Philip 'appeared at Azotus' (the ancient Philistine city of Ashdod) and 'kept preaching the gospel to all the cities until he came to Caesarea.' The geographic sweep — from the Negev desert to the coastal plain to Caesarea — is Philip's personal Acts 1:8 journey. Caesarea is where the Cornelius breakthrough will occur in Acts 10 (Peter's base) and where Paul will eventually be imprisoned (Acts 23-26). The whole Acts narrative is weaving its missionary threads toward Rome; Philip's arrival in Caesarea is an early knot in that web. Christ's mission moves through ordinary travel along existing roads, using the same infrastructure that carries the Roman empire.
